primary_diagnosis.name: Fibromyalgia (centralized/secondary pain) overlapping active mild rheumatoid arthritis

pathophysiology_summary: Central sensitization amplifies nociceptive signalling so that widespread pain, allodynia and fatigue are experienced out of proportion to peripheral joint pathology. On a background of established RA, a low-grade synovitis flare (mild symmetric MCP synovial thickening/warmth) provides a genuine but limited inflammatory driver, while the dominant, disabling symptom burden is centralized (secondary fibromyalgia). The alarming triage framing (immobility, swollen hands, profound lethargy) mimics an acute destructive or septic crisis, but the polyarticular, mildly inflammatory, afebrile, symmetric pattern with preserved power and diffuse tender points is the discriminating story. Methotrexate immunosuppression modestly lowers the threshold to exclude septic arthritis, which remains the key not-to-miss alternative if a single joint becomes disproportionately hot and swollen.

differential.evidence_basis: Swollen joints + immunosuppression demand active exclusion of joint sepsis before anything else, even though the story sounds polyarticular.
Widespread pain with allodynia, fatigue and fog on a chronic background is the most likely explanation.
Known RA with symmetric MCP swelling and prolonged morning stiffness indicates a genuine synovitis component.
Multisystem autoimmune flare can mimic; screen for malar rash, serositis, renal features.
Girdle pain/stiffness overlaps but distribution here is far wider.
Afebrile, symmetric mild synovitis, allodynia and tender points confirm a centralized pain flare over low-grade RA.
Mild MCP synovitis and inflammatory stiffness are real and warrant DMARD review.
Lower probability given no single hot joint and no high fever, but must remain safety-netted given immunosuppression.
No new malar rash, serositis or renal symptoms.
Widespread distal involvement is against PMR.
Working telehealth diagnosis: centralized pain flare over mild RA synovitis; confirm with in-person joint exam and CRP/ESR, and exclude sepsis if any single joint becomes disproportionately inflamed.

red_flags.description: A single joint becoming intensely hot, red, and maximally swollen, or high fever (>38.5 C) with rigors
red_flags.clinical_significance: These would signal septic arthritis in an immunosuppressed patient — a joint-destroying, limb- and life-threatening emergency requiring urgent aspiration.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed septic arthritis leads to rapid cartilage destruction, sepsis and possible death.

red_flags.description: Dehydration from being unable to reach fluids while living alone and bedbound
red_flags.clinical_significance: Immobility plus inadequate intake risks dehydration and (with methotrexate) renal compromise; a social-safety as well as clinical concern.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration/AKI and an unsafe home situation for someone living alone.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting the triage framing of an 'acute rheumatological crisis' and escalating to de-novo systemic steroids and potent analgesia without examining the joints or excluding sepsis.
Attributing all of the disabling pain to joint inflammation and missing the dominant centralized (fibromyalgia) component, leading to over-immunosuppression.
Assuming 'no fever' fully excludes septic arthritis in an immunosuppressed patient, when a single hot joint could still be septic and warrants safety-netting.
Prescribing opioids or new oral steroids over chat in response to the patient's distress and explicit request.

risk_scores.tool_name: Widespread Pain Index / centralized pain (ACR fibromyalgia criteria concept)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Pattern consistent with centralized pain (widespread pain + fatigue + cognitive symptoms)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports a centralized-pain management approach rather than escalating immunosuppression.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Joint palpation for synovial thickening, warmth, effusion, and whether any single joint is disproportionately hot/red (key to excluding septic arthritis)
CRP, ESR and other inflammatory markers
Synovial fluid aspiration and culture if a septic joint is suspected
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange prompt in-person GP/rheumatology review to examine joints and obtain inflammatory markers; direct to ED urgently only if a single joint becomes hot/red/maximally swollen or high fever with rigors develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: De-novo systemic steroids or opioids issued over chat could mask evolving joint sepsis and cause harm without addressing the centralized pain.

guidelines_referenced.reference: EULAR revised recommendations for the management of fibromyalgia (2016)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Fibromyalgia management should prioritise non-pharmacological measures (education, graded exercise, pacing) with low-dose adjuncts such as amitriptyline; opioids are not recommended.
A hot, acutely swollen joint in an immunosuppressed patient requires urgent aspiration to exclude septic arthritis before treating as a non-infective flare.
